course_description: This course introduces students to the Australian legal system and develops student knowledge of Australian business and corporations law. The course is intended to provide a foundation for non-legal professionals intending to work in business.

requirements.restrictions: Course is not recommended for students with legal qualifications
requirements.incompatible: (LAWS1100 + LAWS3100) or LAWS4112 or LAWS5017 or (LAWS7000 + LAWS7011) or (LAWS7010 + LAWS7011) or LAWS7117 or LAWS7234 or LAWS7317 Not available to students in the LLM, LLM(Adv), LLM(#16) or LLM(#24) programs
requirements.assumed_background: No assumed background in legal studies is required. This course is not recommended for students with legal qualifications.

timetable:
Timetables are available on the UQ Public Timetable.

Additional Timetable Information
Please enrol for a seminar that best fits in with your commitments as soon as possible.

Next 2. Aims
course_aims: The broad aim of LAWS7023 is to give you sufficient knowledge and understanding of Australian business and corporate law to recognise, resolve and avoid legal problems in your business, commercial and corporate dealings.

learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate an acceptable level of knowledge and understanding of the elements of Australian business and corporate law.
Communicate your knowledge of Australian business and corporate law effectively and appropriately, in writing and verbally, to your instructors and to your fellow students.
Navigate independently through legal information environments, including online legal resources.
Use your knowledge of Australian business and corporate law to identify, analyse and solve simple legal problems.
Appreciate your legal, social and professional responsibilities to your peers, your profession and your community.

assessment_summary.title: Online Invigilated Corporate Law Final Exam

assessment_details.title: Assessable Tutorial Submissions
assessment_details.task_description:
This assessment task requires submission of a two to three page (A4) answer to Assessable Tutorial Problem Submissions scheduled for a tutorial during the semester at scheduled times during the semester (see Learning Activities). Each of the five (5) submissions are problem questions using the IL&AC technique and are worth two (2) marks each (Five submissions at 2% each totalling 10% of the overall course mark). The two (2) marks will be allocated for the identification and treatment of the issues relevant to the solution of each submission. Further details about the submissions will be provided in t utorials and on Blackboard during the semester. The submissions will be problem questions and students must make a serious and reasonable attempt to answer each submission.

Please take note of the following:

The answer for each submission will be discussed in the relevant Tutorial itself. Tutorials are not recorded and whilst brief guidelines about the answer to each submission will be released at the end of each week, these guidelines will not be a full and formative answer to a submission - students must attend the scheduled tutorials during the semester each week to obtain the full benefit of the discussion since this will aid them in their understanding of both the subject material and answer technique;

At the end of the semester, a mark out of 10 will be given to each student if, in the overall judgement of the course coordinator, the criteria set out in the ECP have been satisfied.

Student answers to submissions must be typed and must be submitted electronically in Word in the relevant submission folder in the course Blackboard site. Students only need to submit their final draft answer for submissions.

Note: Students must ONLY source and use material for their submissions from the LAWS7023 Semester 2 2022 course material - being either the lecture slides, notes taken during the lectures and/or from the lecture recordings, notes taken during the tutorials and/or any material posted under the learning resources teaching week folders. Students are NOT allowed to source material for their submissions that is found outside of the LAWS 7023 Semester 2 2022 course material.

assessment_details.title: Business Law Problem Question Submission
assessment_details.task_description:
A 15% Business Law Problem Question submission containing one IL&AC problem question and one pretend student answer based on Business Law. Students will be required to provide their commentary on the pretend student's answer and students will be required to write their own IL&AC answer to the problem question. Any of the Business Law topics from the lectures and the tutorials may be assessed in the submission.

This Problem Question Submission will be released in Teaching Week 5 and it will be due for submission online in Teaching Week 6 in the 15% Business Law Assignment folder in the Assessments page on Blackboard at 2pm on Thursday 1 September 2022 in Teaching Week 6 ( Written answers must be submitted in the submission folder and cannot be submitted by email or otherwise) . 15% Problem Question Submissions submitted after 2pm 1 September 2022 will be submitted into the Late Submissions folder and will incur Late Penalties - see paragraph 5.3 of the ECP regarding late submissions of assessment.

Students will also be required to submit 3 drafts of their assignment - being their Research Draft, their Second ILAC Draft and their Final Submission Draft - only the Final Submission Draft will be assessed. Group Feedback will be provided to the Problem Question Submission. Further details about the Problem Question Submission will be provided in tutorials and on Blackboard during the semester.

Note : Students must ONLY source and use material for their submissions from the LAWS7023 Semester 2 2022 course material - being either the lecture slides, notes taken during the lectures and/or from the lecture recordings, notes taken during the tutorials and/or any material posted under the learning resources teaching week folders. Students are NOT allowed to source material for their submissions that is found outside of the LAWS7023 Semester 2 2022 course material.

assessment_details.title: Business Law Assignment
assessment_details.task_description:
A 25% Business Law assignment containing one IL&AC problem question based on Business Law. Students will be required to own IL&AC answer to the problem question. Any of the Business Law topics from the lectures and the tutorials may be assessed in the assignment.

The assignment question will be released in Teaching Week 7 and it will be due for submission online in Teaching Week 9 in the 25% Business Law Assignment folder in the Assessments page on Blackboard at 2pm on Friday 23 September 2022 ( Written answers must be submitted in the submission folder and cannot be submitted by email or otherwise) . Assignments submitted after 2pm Friday 23 September 2022 will be submitted into the Late Submissions folder and will incur Late Penalties - see paragraph 5.3 of the ECP regarding late submissions of assessment.

Students will also be required to submit 3 drafts of their assignment - being their Research Draft, their Second ILAC Draft and their Final Submission Draft - only the Final Submission Draft will be assessed. Group Feedback will be provided to the Assignment. Further details about the assignment will be provided in tutorials and on Blackboard during the semester.

assessment_details.title: Online Invigilated Corporate Law Final Exam
assessment_details.task_description:
This final exam will occur during the UQ Final Exam Period and students will have 2 working hours and 10 minutes planning time to complete it.

The 50% Corporations Law Final Assessment will be an open book exam. Any additional written / printed material and access to files saved on the computer are permitted; material may also be annotated. However access to internet resources and using the translation software are not allowed. Students are allowed to use a hardcopy bilingual dictionary. The exam will consist of problem based questions using the IL&AC technique. All corporate law topics may be assessed. Further details about the final exam will be provided in lectures, tutorials and on Blackboard during the semester.

The exam will be conducted online using Inspera Assessment . Inspera is a cloud based assessment platform providing additional functionality to complement the existing suite of digital learning tools at UQ. For more information about Inspera Assessment and to see what the interface looks like, please see https://web.library.uq.edu.au/library-services/it/assessments-inspera and try out the Inspera demo test here. Remember, Inspera is a university-wide platform, so there may be some question types in the demo test that are not relevant to your discipline. Further information will be provided in the Blackboard site during the semester, and a practice opportunity will be available.

Inspera Exam Portal runs on your device and when you start your exam it secures your workstation and restricts access to unauthorised applications, websites and resources. You will be guided through a system check to ensure your device is ready to take the exam. You validate your identity by using a webcam to take a picture of yourself and your identification. A recording of your exam session will be captured on your desktop and uploaded to the Inspera platform at the conclusion of your exam. The recording can only be reviewed by UQ staff that have access to your particular exam instance.

In the event you do not have a valid UQ student ID card, a student may produce a government issued identification document.

Note: Students must ONLY source and use material for the final exam from the LAWS7023 Semester 2 2022 course material - being either the lecture slides, notes taken during the lectures and/or from the lecture recordings, notes taken during the tutorials and/or any material posted under the learning resources teaching week folders. Students are NOT allowed to source material for the final exam that is found outside of the LAWS7023 Semester 2 2022 course material.

learning_resources.required_resources: Material published via the Course Blackboard Site
learning_resources.university_learning_resources:
Access to required and recommended resources, plus past central exam papers, is available at the UQ Library website ( http://www.library.uq.edu.au/lr/LAWS7023 ).

The University offers a range of resources and services to support student learning. Details are available on the myUQ website ( https://my.uq.edu.au/ ).
learning_resources.school_learning_resources:
Law School Citation Style

Students preparing assignments, research papers, dissertations and theses are required to follow the Australian Guide to Legal Citation (4th edition) .

For a short summary of the complete Australian Guide to Legal Citation , see the UQ Library's Referencing Guide to the AGLC .

Law Library

The Law Library's Legal Research Guide site at http://guides.library.uq.edu.au/legal-research-guide provides links to a wide range of Australian and International sites relevant to the study of law.

Students should refer to the UQ Library website generally for information relating to facilities, collections and services available - http://www.library.uq.edu.au .

Course Learn. UQ (Blackboard) Website

Course Learn.UQ (Blackboard) websites contain course announcements; the course profile; the learning guide (where applicable); lecture slides and discussion boards. Students should visit their course Learn.UQ (Blackboard) websites regularly to check for course announcements and to read and contribute to any online activities.

Course Coordinators will maintain and regularly update course Learn.UQ (Blackboard) websites, which can be accessed via the following link – https://learn.uq.edu.au/ . It is your responsibility to ensure that you have access to the websites . Access to Learn.UQ (Blackboard) is automatically generated for those students enrolled through my SI-net approximately 1 week prior to the course commencing. If you do not have access by that time please contact the AskUs Library Help – askus@library.uq.edu.au .

The University of Queensland Library offers computing help and training free to UQ students - https://web.library.uq.edu.au/library-services/it .

Previous 2. Aims

learning_activities.topic: Mon - Fri Principles of Law and the Australian Legal System : Teaching Week 1 Lecture: Starting in Teaching Week 1 and continuing through to Teaching Week 12, there will be a weekly lecture which all students must attend - internal students can attend lectures in person and external students can attend lectures online via zoom. The lecture in Teaching Week 1 (TW1) will provide an overview of the course including how to do well in this course, the importance of summarising the subject material on a weekly basis and the three draft strategy for tutorial submissions, the problem question submission and the assignment. This lecture will also present the TW1 topic Introduction to Australia's Legal system which realtes to the non-assessable tutorial question submission - which will be further discussed at length in the Teaching Week 1 tutorials. Students must read the lecture material before attending the lecture, attend the lecture and take notes, then listen to the lecture recording and summarise the lecture and other teaching week material this week. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Blackboard

Mon - Fri Non-Assessable Tutorial this Week (TW1) : There will be tutorials occuring during certain weeks during the semester which all students must also attend - internal students can attend internal tutorials in person and external students can attend external tutorials online via zoom. Tutorials discuss the answers to assessable tutorial problem submissions and the course content. As an introduction to the course, this tutorial in Teaching Week 1 will be non-assessable and will follow on from the TW1 lecture with a visual workshop on problem question technique and some discussion and guidance on a non-assessable ILAC question which students must submit online by 9am Tuesday 2 August 2022 (TW2). Students must attend the tutorial, take notes and summarise the tutorial discussion this week. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5

Mon - Fri Formation of Contracts - Contracts 1 : Teaching Week 2 Lecture: This lecture will introduce the basic concepts of contract law including, offer, acceptance and consideration. Students must read the lecture material before attending the lecture, attend the lecture and take notes, then listen to the lecture recording and summarise the lecture and other teaching week material this week. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Blackboard

Mon - Fri Non-Assessable Tutorial This Week (TW2) : Non-Assessable Tutorial Submission question due online 9am Tuesday 2 August. Continuation of the TW1 Non-Assessable Tutorial with discussion of Non-Assessable Tutorial Submission. Release of the First Assessable Tutorial Submission (2%) due online 9am Tuesday 16 August (TW4). Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Blackboard

Mon - Fri Enforcing Contracts - Contracts 2 : Teaching Week 3 Lecture: This lecture will examine when a contract is enforceable and when it is invalid and unenforceable. Students must read the lecture material before attending the lecture, attend the lecture and take notes, then listen to the lecture recording and summarise the lecture and other teaching week material this week. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Blackboard

Mon - Fri No Tutorials This Week (TW3) : Public Holiday Wednesday 10 August: Students are encouraged to summarise this weeks lecture material. Students are encouraged to keep working on the First Assessable Tutorial Submission (2%) due online 9am Tuesday 16 August (TW4). Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5

Mon - Fri Causing Harm - Torts : Teaching Week 4 Lecture: This lecture will examine the basic concepts of tort law including negligence, duty of care, breach of duty of care and causation. Students must read the lecture material before attending the lecture, attend the lecture and take notes, then listen to the lecture recording and summarise the lecture and other teaching week material this week. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Blackboard

Mon - Fri Tutorials This Week (TW4) : First Assessable Tutorial Submission due online 9am Tuesday 16 August - Discussion of First Assessable Tutorial Submission in the TW4 tutorials. Release of the Second Assessable Tutorial Submission (2%) due online 9am Tuesday 23 August (TW5). Students must attend the tutorial, take notes and summarise the tutorial discussion this week Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Blackboard

Mon - Fri Consumers and Anti-Competitive Behaviour Law : Teaching Week 5 Lecture: This lecture will examine consumer law in Australia, in particular the consumer protection provisions of the Competition and Consumer Act as well as examining the anti-competitive behaviour provisions. Students must read the lecture material before attending the lecture, attend the lecture and take notes, then listen to the lecture recording and summarise the lecture and other teaching week material this week. Mon - Sun Tutorials This Week (TW5) : Second Assessable Tutorial Submission due online 9am Tuesday 23 August - Discussion of Second Assessable Tutorial Submission in the TW5 tutorials. Release of the Problem Question Submission and presentation on the Problem Question Submission in the Tutorial.Students must attend the tutorial, take notes and summarise the tutorial discussion this week. Mon - Fri 15% Problem Question Submission Released (Assessable) : Students will have to submit a detailed commentary on a pretend students answer and students will also have to prepare their own answer to a problem question and submit both items online in Teaching Week 6 by 2pm Thursday 1 September 2022. Mon - Fri Intellectual Property : Teaching Week 6 Lecture: This lecture will look at Intellectual Property (IP) Law, being the set of rules that protect IP from unauthorised use and exploitation by others. policies_and_procedures:
University Policies & Guidelines

An overview of the University's assessment-related procedures can be found on my.UQ. ( https://my.uq.edu.au/services/exams-and-assessment )

Academic Integrity

It is the University's task to encourage ethical scholarship and to inform students and staff about the institutional standards of academic behaviour expected of them in learning, teaching and research. Students have a responsibility to maintain the highest standards of academic integrity in their work. Students must not cheat in examinations or other forms of assessment and must ensure they do not plagiarise.

Academic Integrity Modules - Support for Students

It may seem simple – don’t cheat and do reference your work. However, there are many issues involved in understanding what is meant by academic integrity and how you could be misled into engaging in an action which may be academic misconduct. The Academic Integrity Modules (AIM) will enable you to uphold academic integrity throughout your studies at UQ and beyond. From Semester 1, 2021, completion of the modules in your first semester of enrolment will be compulsory for all commencing students.

Plagiarism

The University has adopted the following definition of plagiarism:

Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

Students are encouraged to read the UQ Student Integrity and Misconduct policy ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.60.04-student-integrity-and-misconduct ) which makes a comprehensive statement about the University's approach to plagiarism, including the approved use of plagiarism detection software, the consequences of plagiarism and the principles associated with preventing plagiarism.

Applications for Extensions

An application for an Extension of Assessment Due Date for medical grounds and/or other exceptional circumstances shall be submitted by lodging the appropriate (online) form with supporting documentation by the date specified in Section 5.3 of the Electronic Course Profile. Students will be notified of the outcome of their request via the myRequests section of my.UQ (and an email sent to their student account).

Additional details associated with extension requests, including acceptable and unacceptable reasons, may be found at my.UQ .

Request for Deferral of Exams

A request for deferral of an exam must be submitted by lodging the appropriate (online) form with supporting documentation via mySI-net > my Requests no later than five (5) calendar days after the date of the original exam. Students are encouraged to familiarise themselves with the information on deferring an exam available on my.UQ .

Feedback on Assessment

There are certain steps you can take if you feel your result does not reflect your performance. Please refer to the my.UQ web site. ( https://my.uq.edu.au/information-and-services/manage-my-program/academic-progress-and-final-results/querying-result )

As a student you have a responsibility to incorporate feedback into your learning; make use of the assessment criteria that you are given; be aware of the rules, policies and other documents related to assessment; and provide teachers with feedback on their assessment practices.

Supplementary assessment

A supplementary assessment is designed to give you a second chance at achieving a passing grade for a course, should you receive a final course grade of 3 (or N subject to the Supplementary Assessment Procedures ). It isn't a repeat of an exam, or a specific assignment – it's designed to test the learning outcomes for the entire course, or specific learning outcomes tailored to you.

Supplementary assessment can take any form (e.g. oral, written, examination etc.), and the highest grade you can receive is a 4 (or P). Supplementary assessment isn't available for every course and is only granted in accordance with the Supplementary Assessment Procedures .

Please check the course profile to see if your course is fully or partially exempt from supplementary assessment. If you have any questions about supplementary assessment, or if you're unsure whether you qualify, please contact staff in your school office or faculty office.

Course grade cut-offs

Any change to grade cut-offs will be in accordance with PPL 3.10.02 Assessment – Procedures .

Feedback in this Course

There are certain steps you can take if you feel your result does not reflect your performance. Please refer to the my.UQ web site. ( https://my.uq.edu.au/information-and-services/manage-my-program/academic-progress-and-final-results/querying-result )

As a student you have a responsibility to incorporate feedback into your learning; make use of the assessment criteria that you are given; be aware of the rules, policies and other documents related to assessment; and provide teachers with feedback on their assessment practices.

School of Law
Assessment Guidelines

Communication with Examiners

Students who have completed an item of assessment should not contact the examiners about that item of assessment prior to the release of the grades or marks for that item of assessment. For queries of an administrative nature, please contact Law School Reception.

After the release of the final grades for the course, feedback may be sought on examination performance in accordance with the School’s policy ( https://law.uq.edu.au/files/7645/TL14-Feedback-on-final-examinations.pdf ).

Deferred Examinations ( mid-semester and end of semester )

Where a student does not attend an examination, the student will in the normal course of events receive zero marks for the examination.

In accordance with Section 6 of PPL 3.10.11 Examinations – Procedures , permission to sit a deferred examination will only be granted in the event of a certified medical condition or other exceptional circumstances which prevented attendance at the examination. Note – except in truly exceptional circumstances, a student who attends whole or part of the original examination will not be eligible for a deferred examination.

Requests for deferral of an examination must be submitted online via mySI-net (myRequests tab) within 5 calendar days following the date of the examination. Supporting documentation must be attached to the request.

Deferred mid-semester examinations will be held in the period nominated by the School for the holding of mid-semester examinations. Deferred end of semester examinations will be held during the University’s deferred examination period.

Students should refer to my.UQ for further information relating to eligibility and how to request a deferred examination.

University Policies and Guidelines

Placement Courses

Students on a placement course – also known as a work placement, internship, industry study, industry experience, clinical practice, clinical placement, practical work, practicum, fieldwork, teaching practice – should refer to the University’s Work Integrated Learning and Work Experience Policy ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.04-work-integrated-learning-and-work-experience ) for detailed information.

Working with Children

Students whose studies include a professional/work placement, internship, clinical practice, teaching practice or other similar activity which involves them in regular contact with children should refer to the University policy, Working with Children Check - "blue card" ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/1.60.07-working-children ) to find out how to apply for a ‘blue card’.

Students with a Disability

Any student with a disability who may require alternative academic arrangements, including assessment, in the course/program is encouraged to seek advice at the commencement of the semester from a Disability Advisor at Student Services. Refer to the University policy, Students with a Disability (Disability Action Plan) ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.08-alternative-academic-arrangements-students-disability ) and to the policy on Special Arrangements for Examinations for Students with a Disability ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.09-arrangements-reasonable-adjustments-examinations-students-disability#Procedures ).

While it is the responsibility of the relevant faculty to liaise with professional and registration bodies regarding the acceptability of any adjustment to an academic program, the University Health Service can arrange appropriate advice and assistance on personal and public health issues.

Occupational Health and Safety

Undergraduate and Postgraduate Students should be familiar with the University policies on occupational health and safety in the laboratory ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/2.30.14-occupational-health-and-safety-laboratory ).

Fitness to Practise

Students enrolled in programs and courses that include a practical placement component (also known as work integrated learning, clinical immersion, clinical placement, clinical practice, externship, fieldwork, industry experience, industry study, internship, practicum, teaching practice, work placement) should refer to the University policy Fitness to Practise ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.30.14-fitness-practise ) for detailed information on issues of competence and on management of concerns relating to patient and public safety.

Other
School of Law
Guidelines

Responsibilities of Students

Students are expected to take responsibility for their own learning and academic development. This includes:

devoting sufficient time and effort to your studies to make and maintain adequate progress,

preparing for lectures and tutorials,

reading prescribed materials,

undertaking such independent study as necessary to reach an understanding of the materials,

completion and submission of assessment items on time,

compliance with School, Faculty, and University rules.

Student Workload

For each 2 unit course students are expected to undertake at least 7 – 8 hours per week of independent study in addition to the time spent in lectures and tutorials.

Student and Staff Communication

If you email a staff member directly, you must include in your email your name, your student number and the name of the course you are enquiring about. Courtesy is always appreciated.

If you telephone a staff member directly and leave a message on their voicemail, you must provide contact details other than a mobile telephone number except where this is absolutely unavoidable.

While the use of telephone and email is acceptable for brief communications with a staff member, you should not expect detailed responses to questions about the law. These questions should be raised on the discussion board, in tutorial sessions or in staff consultation sessions.

Law School Website

Students should refer to the Law School website for more information including enrolment information and important dates; student announcements; news and events; information on Law School prizes and scholarships etc – http://www.law.uq.edu.au .

Previous 5. Assessment

Next Summary

graduate_attributes.intro: Successfully completing this course will contribute to the recognition of your attainment of the following UQ (Postgrad Coursework) graduate attributes:

E4 . A knowledge and respect of ethics and ethical standards in relation to a major area of study.

E7 . The ability to work effectively and sensitively across all areas of society .
E8 . An understanding of and respect for the roles and expertise of associated disciplines .

other_learning_activities_information:
The Function of Lectures

Lectures are a forum where the lecturer can:

Present the subject material and explore theoretical and legal concepts relevant to the topic;

Critically evaluate these concepts and explore their relationships to one another;

Identify the central conceptual, intellectual and doctrinal questions raised by the material under consideration;

Place the material discussed into a wider intellectual and practical context.

Lectures afford students an opportunity in understanding the subject content with a view to developing their skills in analytical reasoning and argument. The effectiveness of lectures is dependent upon all students having read the relevant lecture material before attending the lecture, being prepared to actively take notes about what the lecturer says in the lecture and summarising the lecture content in the same week.

Please note that students can attend lectures either in-person or online via zoom as the lecture occurs. Please also note that the lecture will be recorded and a recording of each weeks lecture will be made available to students on the course website in the same week.

The Function of Tutorials

Tutorials offer students an interactive forum within which to explore in more detail and in greater depth the issues introduced in lectures and to engage with the course coordinator and other students in exploring those issues. Tutorials also offer students an opportunity develop their skills in legal reasoning and argument.

Please note that the tutorials are not recorded - so as an interactive learning forum, the tutorials are dependent upon all students having read and prepared the material to be considered, attending all tutorials during the semester - whether face-to-face or online via Zoom - and being prepared to think about the concepts raised, ask questions and to engage in discussion to enhance and improve student learning and understanding.

course_grading:
Example criteria for each of the grades can be found in PPL 3.10.02 Assessment Procedures - section 7 Appendix.

Grade X: No assessable work received.

Grade 1, Low Fail: Absence of evidence of achievement of course learning outcomes:

0% - 19%

Grade 2, Fail: Minimal evidence of achievement of course learning outcomes:

20% - 47%

Grade 3, Marginal Fail: Demonstrated evidence of developing achievement of course learning outcomes:

48% - 49%

Grade 4, Pass: Demonstrated evidence of functional achievement of course learning outcomes:

50% - 64%

Grade 5, Credit: Demonstrated evidence of proficient achievement of course learning outcomes:

65% - 74%

Grade 6, Distinction: Demonstrated evidence of advanced achievement of course learning outcomes:

75% - 84%

Grade 7, High Distinction: Demonstrated evidence of exceptional achievement of course learning outcomes:

85% +

Other Requirements & Comments:

Please Note: There is a STRONG correlation between seminar attendance and assessment performance - All students are strongly encouraged to ensure that they attend EACH and EVERY seminar.
late_submission:
Penalties for Late Submission

Where a non-examination item of assessment (eg essays, assignments etc) is not submitted by the due date and time, a penalty will be levied at the rate of 10% of the marks available for the assessment item per day that the assessment is overdue. The date and time of submission will be deemed to be that noted on the electronic submission. Assessment submitted more than 7 days after the due date and time will receive zero marks.

Example : Where a piece of assessment is due at 2pm on Monday, assessment submitted after 2pm on Monday but before 2pm on Tuesday will receive a 10% penalty.

Example : Where a piece of assessment is due at 2pm on the 10th, any work submitted after 2pm on the 17th will receive zero marks.

Extension to Assessment Due Date

Extensions will not normally exceed 14 days. If you have been ill or unable to attend class for more than 14 days, we advise you to consider carefully whether you are capable of successfully completing your courses this semester. You might be eligible to withdraw without academic penalty.

Spoken or written notification of difficulties with assessment deadlines to a course coordinator or the School does not constitute an authorised extension.

Reasons for extension and evidence :

• If the basis of the application is a medical condition:

o Applications must be accompanied by a medical certificate dated on or before the assignment due date.

o It is your responsibility to ensure that the provider of your medical certificate is a genuine medical practitioner. You will be held fully responsible for whatever you submit, even if you do so in apparent ignorance. It is therefore your responsibility to ensure that anything you submit is authentic.

o An extension granted to a student on medical grounds will not exceed the number of days suffered from a medical condition, as stated on the medical certificate. The medical condition must have occurred within the period two weeks prior to the advertised due date of the assignment.

• If the basis of the application is some other circumstance:

o The circumstances must have occurred within the period two weeks prior to the advertised due date of the assignment; and

o the extension granted will be in proportion to the period of disruption caused by the circumstances, and be no more than two weeks in duration.

Please refer to the Applying for an extension page on my.UQ for further details on acceptable reasons for an extension and for instructions on how to apply .

Applications to be submitted before the due date : Applications for extension (whether they be medical or exceptional circumstances) shall be made by the due date and time for the assessment . Requests for extensions received after the assessment item submission due date will not be accepted unless there is evidence to demonstrate extenuating circumstances that made it impossible to submit the request earlier. Late requests received by the School will require the decision maker listed in section 2.3.2 of PPL 3.60.02 Student Grievance Resolution to accept the request for consideration.
other_assessment_information:
Supplementary Assessment

Should you fail a course with a grade of 3, or a non-graded ‘N’, you may be eligible for supplementary assessment. Refer to my.UQ for information on supplementary assessment.

Supplementary assessment may not be available for all courses, or for some of the assessment items for a course. The highest grade you can receive following supplementary assessment is a 4 or P. Details of availability of supplementary assessment for this course are set out below.

Supplementary Assessment is available for this Course .

Refer to my.UQ for how to apply for supplementary assessment.

Supplementary assessment can take any form, for example, an oral or a written exam. Students who are eligible and approved for a supplementary assessment and the form of assessment is an examination, are expected to be available to sit the supplementary exam during the University’s Deferred and Supplementary examination period. Once approved, supplementary assessment cannot be rescinded by the student.
